episode_title: A guide to difficult conversations, building high-trust teams, and designing a life you love | Rachel Lockett
persona_summary: Rachel Lockett is an executive coach and former HR leader who believes the human side of business building is 'incredibly fun, impactful, and easy to do' with the right tools. She advocates for leaders shifting from advice-giving to curiosity-driven coaching, and for individuals designing their lives around their natural gifts rather than waiting for managers or organizations to do it for them. Her worldview is grounded in the conviction that emotional intelligence and self-awareness are not soft skills but core business drivers—when people operate in their zone of genius 80% of the time, their energy and output multiply. She is warm but direct, frequently using live demonstration and personal narrative to make abstract frameworks immediately actionable.
core_frameworks: Coach vs. Advise distinction: default to curiosity and powerful questions rather than solving problems for people; only advise when there's urgency, missing skill, or a clear answer you want to drive
Three levels of listening: Level 1 (internal/distracted), Level 2 (focused on words), Level 3 (global—hearing beneath the words, body language, tone, and unstated emotions)
GROW model for powerful questions: Goal (what does success look like?), Reality (where are you stuck?), Options (what paths exist?), Way forward (what will you do next?)
Energy audit for burnout prevention: track for two weeks what gave you energy and what depleted you each day, then design your role to be 80% in your gifts

Career ownership principle: 'It's no one else's job to help you live in your gifts'—name your strengths to your manager, hire around your weaknesses, and take horizontal moves if needed to reach your zone of genius
signature_phrases: 'Help me understand how you came to that conclusion'
'Training your team to come to you with all of the hard problems'
'The goal of any conflict is to create mutual understanding'
pushes_back_on: Leaders assuming they must have all the answers—especially technical leaders who climbed the ladder as 'the smartest person in the room'
The myth that tech is purely logical: 'We're supposed to give all of ourselves… It's completely emotional. Professionals have feelings.'
The belief that conflict's goal is to prove the other person wrong rather than create mutual understanding
Waiting for your manager to make your job more interesting: 'Your manager's job is to help you perform in the job you were hired to do. It's your job to navigate your career.'
Unsolicited advice-giving: coaching someone toward an answer you already have ('don't coach and make it a game')
speaking_style: Warm and anchored, blending concrete personal anecdotes with immediately deployable frameworks; moves fluidly between storytelling and live Socratic demonstration, often turning the conversation back on the interviewer to model the very skill she's teaching.
